title: Feedback Loops in the Endocrine System

quiz_questions.question_text: Most endocrine regulation uses:

options.text: Positive feedback
No feedback mechanism
Negative feedback
Random uncontrolled secretion

quiz_questions.explanation: Negative feedback is the primary regulatory mechanism for the vast majority of hormone systems, working to keep hormone levels within a narrow physiological range by inhibiting further release when levels rise.

quiz_questions.question_text: In negative feedback, the product of the pathway:

options.text: Amplifies the original stimulus to produce even more product
Has no effect on its own production
Permanently destroys the producing gland
Inhibits the stimulus, reducing further production

quiz_questions.explanation: In negative feedback, the end product of a hormonal pathway feeds back to inhibit earlier steps in the pathway, effectively shutting down its own production once adequate levels have been reached.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid (HPT) axis, high T3/T4 levels:

options.text: Inhibit TRH and TSH release, reducing thyroid stimulation
Directly stimulate the thyroid to produce even more hormones
Have no effect on the hypothalamus or pituitary
Stimulate more TSH and TRH production

quiz_questions.explanation: When T3 and T4 levels are sufficient, they feed back to inhibit both TRH from the hypothalamus and TSH from the anterior pituitary, reducing further thyroid stimulation and preventing hormone excess.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, high cortisol:

options.text: Directly destroys the adrenal glands
Has no regulatory feedback on its own axis
Inhibits CRH and ACTH, reducing cortisol production
Stimulates more CRH release from the hypothalamus

quiz_questions.explanation: Elevated cortisol exerts negative feedback at both the hypothalamic (suppressing CRH) and pituitary (suppressing ACTH) levels, thereby reducing further adrenal stimulation and cortisol production.

quiz_questions.question_text: An example of positive feedback in the endocrine system is:

options.text: Oxytocin during labor, where contractions increase oxytocin which causes stronger contractions
Cortisol regulation during chronic stress
TSH regulation of the thyroid
Insulin reducing blood glucose after a meal

quiz_questions.explanation: Oxytocin during childbirth is a classic positive feedback example: uterine contractions stimulate oxytocin release, which increases contraction strength, which stimulates even more oxytocin, escalating until delivery occurs.

quiz_questions.question_text: The LH surge that triggers ovulation is an example of:

options.text: Negative feedback by estrogen on the pituitary
Constant steady-state secretion of LH
No feedback involvement at all
Positive feedback, where rising estrogen triggers a spike in LH release

quiz_questions.explanation: During the menstrual cycle, when estrogen levels reach a critical threshold, they switch from negative to positive feedback on the anterior pituitary, causing a massive surge in LH secretion that triggers ovulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Positive feedback loops end when:

options.text: They never end and continue indefinitely
The stimulus is removed, such as when the baby is delivered and oxytocin release stops
Negative feedback permanently replaces them
They automatically reverse after exactly 24 hours

quiz_questions.explanation: Positive feedback loops are self-amplifying and require removal of the initiating stimulus to terminate; for example, delivery of the baby removes cervical pressure, ending the oxytocin positive feedback cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the thyroid gland is surgically removed, TSH levels would:

options.text: Rise, because there is no T3/T4 to provide negative feedback
Drop significantly
Stay exactly the same
Disappear completely from the blood

quiz_questions.explanation: Without the thyroid gland, T3 and T4 production ceases, removing the negative feedback signal to the pituitary; consequently, TSH levels rise dramatically as the pituitary attempts to stimulate a gland that is no longer present.

quiz_questions.question_text: Hormonal rhythms such as cortisol being highest in the morning are called:

options.text: Monthly cycles only
Random fluctuations with no pattern
Circadian rhythms
Seasonal affective patterns

quiz_questions.explanation: Circadian rhythms are approximately 24-hour biological cycles regulated by the suprachiasmatic nucleus; cortisol follows a circadian pattern with peak levels in the early morning to prepare the body for the day's activities.

quiz_questions.question_text: Exogenous steroid use can suppress the HPA axis because:

options.text: Steroids stimulate more CRH production
Steroids have no effect on the HPA axis
Exogenous steroids are not absorbed by the body
External cortisol provides negative feedback, reducing natural CRH, ACTH, and cortisol production

quiz_questions.explanation: When a patient takes synthetic corticosteroids, the exogenous cortisol provides constant negative feedback to the hypothalamus and pituitary, suppressing CRH and ACTH production, which leads to adrenal atrophy over time and the risk of adrenal crisis if steroids are stopped suddenly.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a negative feedback loop?

options.text: A feedback system that only operates during sleep
A one-way signal from the brain to the endocrine gland with no return information
A system where the output amplifies the original stimulus to produce more output
A regulatory mechanism in which the output of a process inhibits the input, maintaining the variable near a set point

quiz_questions.explanation: A negative feedback loop is a homeostatic mechanism in which the end product of a pathway inhibits the initial stimulus or earlier steps, preventing overproduction and maintaining the regulated variable within a narrow range around its set point.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a positive feedback loop?

options.text: A feedback loop that only produces beneficial outcomes
A mechanism in which the output enhances or amplifies the original stimulus, driving the process to completion
A mechanism that maintains variables at a constant level
The same thing as negative feedback but in the opposite direction

quiz_questions.explanation: A positive feedback loop amplifies the initiating stimulus rather than opposing it, creating an escalating cycle that continues until an external event terminates the loop, as seen in childbirth (oxytocin) and the LH surge during ovulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'set point' mean in homeostasis?

options.text: The minimum value needed for survival only
The ideal physiological value that the body's feedback mechanisms work to maintain, such as 37 degrees Celsius for body temperature
A random value that changes daily without regulation
The maximum tolerable value before organ failure occurs

quiz_questions.explanation: A set point is the target value for a physiological parameter that feedback loops maintain, such as normal body temperature (approximately 37 degrees C), blood glucose (around 70-100 mg/dL fasting), or blood pH (7.35-7.45).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is TRH?

options.text: Thyroid resistance hormone, which blocks thyroid activity
Thyrotropin-releasing hormone, produced by the hypothalamus to stimulate TSH release from the anterior pituitary
Thyroid receptor hormone, found on thyroid cells
Total thyroid hormone, a measure of T3 and T4 combined

quiz_questions.explanation: TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone) is a tripeptide hormone secreted by the hypothalamus into the hypophyseal portal system, stimulating thyrotroph cells of the anterior pituitary to produce and secrete TSH, which then acts on the thyroid gland.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is CRH?

options.text: Cortisol-receptor hormone found on adrenal cells
Corticotropin-releasing hormone, produced by the hypothalamus to stimulate ACTH release from the anterior pituitary
Central regulatory hormone from the cerebral cortex
Calcium-related hormone from the parathyroid glands

quiz_questions.explanation: CRH (corticotropin-releasing hormone) is a 41-amino-acid peptide secreted by the hypothalamus in response to stress, stimulating the anterior pituitary to release ACTH, which then stimulates the adrenal cortex to produce cortisol.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is GnRH?

options.text: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone, produced by the hypothalamus to stimulate FSH and LH release from the anterior pituitary
General neuroendocrine hormone from the cerebellum
Glucose-regulating hormone from the pancreas
Growth-releasing hormone that stimulates bone growth

quiz_questions.explanation: GnRH (gonadotropin-releasing hormone) is a decapeptide released in a pulsatile manner from the hypothalamus, stimulating the anterior pituitary gonadotrophs to secrete FSH and LH, which then regulate reproductive function.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'circadian rhythm' mean?

options.text: A rhythm that occurs once per month, like menstruation
A heart rhythm abnormality caused by hormone imbalance
A seasonal pattern of hormone release occurring only in winter
An approximately 24-hour biological cycle that regulates physiological processes like hormone secretion, sleep, and body temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Circadian rhythms (from Latin 'circa diem' meaning 'about a day') are endogenous approximately 24-hour cycles controlled by the suprachiasmatic nucleus of the hypothalamus, regulating hormone release patterns, sleep-wake cycles, body temperature, and many other physiological functions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'HPA axis' stand for?

options.text: Hormonal positive amplification axis, a type of positive feedback
Hepatic-pancreatic-adipose axis, regulating fat metabolism
High-pressure arterial axis, regulating blood flow
Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, the hormonal cascade from hypothalamus to pituitary to adrenal cortex that regulates cortisol

quiz_questions.explanation: The HPA axis refers to the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis: the hypothalamus secretes CRH, which stimulates the anterior pituitary to release ACTH, which stimulates the adrenal cortex to produce cortisol, with cortisol feeding back to inhibit both CRH and ACTH.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'HPG axis' mean?

options.text: Hormonal protein growth axis for muscle development
Hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, the hormonal cascade that regulates reproductive hormones through GnRH, FSH/LH, and sex steroids
High-potency glucocorticoid axis for cortisol regulation
Hepatic-pancreatic-gastric axis for digestive hormone regulation

quiz_questions.explanation: The HPG axis is the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis: the hypothalamus releases GnRH, which stimulates the anterior pituitary to secrete FSH and LH, which act on the gonads to produce sex hormones (estrogen, progesterone, testosterone) that feed back to regulate the axis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a releasing hormone?

options.text: A hormone that triggers the release of calcium from bone
A hormone produced by the hypothalamus that stimulates the anterior pituitary to secrete a specific pituitary hormone
Any hormone that is released into the bloodstream
A hormone that causes cells to release stored glucose

quiz_questions.explanation: Releasing hormones are peptides produced by hypothalamic neurons and secreted into the hypophyseal portal system; they stimulate specific anterior pituitary cell types to synthesize and release their respective hormones, such as TRH stimulating TSH release.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient has been taking prednisone (a synthetic cortisol) daily for six months to manage an autoimmune condition. The physician insists the medication must be tapered slowly rather than stopped abruptly. Based on your knowledge of HPA axis feedback, what is the danger of sudden withdrawal?

options.text: The thyroid gland would shut down permanently
The patient would develop hyperthyroidism from excess TSH
The patient would produce too much cortisol immediately after stopping
Chronic exogenous cortisol has suppressed the HPA axis, so the adrenal glands cannot produce enough cortisol on their own, risking a life-threatening adrenal crisis

quiz_questions.explanation: Long-term exogenous cortisol suppresses the HPA axis through negative feedback, causing CRH and ACTH to decrease and the adrenal cortex to atrophy; sudden withdrawal means the atrophied adrenals cannot produce sufficient cortisol, potentially causing a fatal adrenal crisis with hypotension and shock.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student examines lab results for a patient: TSH is very high, and T4 is very low. The attending physician asks the student to determine whether this is a primary or secondary thyroid problem. What is the correct interpretation?

options.text: Primary hypothyroidism, because the thyroid is failing and the pituitary is compensating by increasing TSH through loss of negative feedback
Hyperthyroidism, because TSH is elevated
Secondary hypothyroidism, because the pituitary is overproducing TSH
Normal thyroid function with a lab error

quiz_questions.explanation: High TSH with low T4 indicates primary hypothyroidism: the thyroid gland itself is failing to produce adequate hormones, so the loss of negative feedback causes the pituitary to compensate by increasing TSH output in an attempt to stimulate the failing gland.

quiz_questions.question_text: A woman in active labor is receiving pitocin (synthetic oxytocin) to strengthen her contractions. A nursing student asks why the contractions keep getting stronger. Which feedback mechanism explains this escalation?

options.text: Positive feedback, because oxytocin stimulates contractions which stimulate more oxytocin release, amplifying the process until delivery
Negative feedback, because the contractions will eventually stop
No feedback, because pitocin is synthetic and bypasses all feedback
Circadian rhythm, because labor typically occurs at night

quiz_questions.explanation: Labor is driven by positive feedback: contractions push the baby against the cervix, stimulating stretch receptors that signal the hypothalamus to release more oxytocin, which causes even stronger contractions, creating an escalating cycle that continues until delivery removes the stimulus.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with chronic unmanaged stress presents with weight gain, elevated blood sugar, immune suppression, and anxiety. The physician explains that the patient's stress response has been chronically activated. Which axis is most directly responsible for these symptoms?

options.text: The insulin-glucagon axis causing excess insulin
The HPT axis causing excess thyroid hormone
The HPG axis causing excess testosterone
The HPA axis causing chronically elevated cortisol

quiz_questions.explanation: Chronic stress persistently activates the HPA axis, leading to sustained high cortisol levels that override normal negative feedback; chronically elevated cortisol causes weight gain (especially central), hyperglycemia, immune suppression, and mood disturbances.

quiz_questions.question_text: An endocrinologist is evaluating a patient with suspected Cushing's disease (an ACTH-secreting pituitary tumor). The patient has high cortisol but does not respond to a dexamethasone suppression test. What does this tell you about the feedback loop?

options.text: The feedback loop is functioning normally
The patient's adrenal glands have been destroyed
The pituitary tumor is autonomously producing ACTH regardless of cortisol feedback, meaning the negative feedback loop has been overridden
The thyroid axis is responsible for the cortisol elevation

quiz_questions.explanation: In Cushing's disease, a pituitary adenoma autonomously secretes ACTH regardless of cortisol levels; the dexamethasone suppression test provides exogenous cortisol-like negative feedback, but the tumor does not respond because it operates independently of normal feedback control.

quiz_questions.question_text: A couple undergoing fertility treatment learns that the woman's estrogen levels have reached a critical threshold mid-cycle. The reproductive endocrinologist explains that estrogen is now having the opposite effect on LH compared to earlier in the cycle. What is happening?

options.text: The pituitary has stopped responding to all hormones
At the critical threshold, estrogen switches from negative to positive feedback on the pituitary, causing the LH surge that triggers ovulation
Estrogen is now suppressing LH even more strongly than before
Estrogen has no effect on LH at any point in the cycle

quiz_questions.explanation: During most of the follicular phase, moderate estrogen levels exert negative feedback on LH; however, when estrogen from the dominant follicle reaches a critical threshold, it triggers a switch to positive feedback, causing a rapid surge in LH that triggers ovulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bodybuilder using anabolic steroids (synthetic testosterone) visits his doctor and is found to have very small testes and a very low sperm count. Using your knowledge of the HPG axis, what explains these findings?

options.text: Exogenous testosterone provides negative feedback to the hypothalamus and pituitary, suppressing GnRH, FSH, and LH, leading to testicular atrophy and reduced sperm production
The steroids directly damaged his testes through toxicity
This is unrelated to the steroids and is a coincidence
The steroids increased FSH and LH production, overstimulating the testes

quiz_questions.explanation: Exogenous testosterone provides strong negative feedback to the HPG axis, suppressing GnRH from the hypothalamus and FSH/LH from the pituitary; without FSH and LH stimulation, the testes atrophy and spermatogenesis dramatically declines.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student notices that a patient's cortisol levels are highest at 8 AM and lowest at midnight. The student asks why cortisol is not constant throughout the day. What is the best explanation?

options.text: The patient has Cushing's syndrome causing irregular cortisol patterns
Cortisol measurements are always unreliable and vary randomly
The patient is more stressed in the morning and relaxed at night
Cortisol follows a normal circadian rhythm regulated by the suprachiasmatic nucleus, peaking in the morning to prepare the body for daily activities

quiz_questions.explanation: Cortisol secretion follows a circadian rhythm controlled by the suprachiasmatic nucleus and the HPA axis, with peak levels in the early morning hours to prepare the body for waking activity and a gradual decline throughout the day to its nadir around midnight.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with an autoimmune condition has both the thyroid and adrenal glands failing simultaneously. The endocrinologist explains that replacement of thyroid hormone must not be started before cortisol replacement. Based on feedback loop knowledge, why is this order important?

options.text: Cortisol blocks thyroid hormone absorption, so it must be given first
The patient would develop hyperthyroidism if given thyroid hormone first
Thyroid hormone has no interaction with cortisol metabolism
Thyroid hormone replacement increases metabolic rate and cortisol demand; without cortisol replacement first, the increased demand on already-failing adrenals could precipitate an adrenal crisis

quiz_questions.explanation: Thyroid hormone increases overall metabolic rate, which increases the body's demand for cortisol; if cortisol is not replaced first, the heightened metabolic demand on already-insufficient adrenal glands can precipitate a life-threatening adrenal crisis.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical researcher is developing a new drug that blocks the enzyme converting angiotensin I to angiotensin II. A colleague asks how this would affect the feedback loop controlling blood pressure. What would be the expected outcome?

options.text: Blood pressure would decrease because blocking angiotensin II formation reduces vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated sodium retention
There would be no effect on blood pressure because angiotensin II is not important
The kidneys would stop producing renin permanently
Blood pressure would increase because angiotensin I directly raises blood pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: Blocking ACE prevents the conversion of angiotensin I to the potent vasoconstrictor angiotensin II; without angiotensin II, blood vessels remain dilated and aldosterone secretion decreases, reducing sodium and water retention and lowering blood pressure. This is exactly how ACE inhibitor drugs work clinically.
